content: Hey, I've been thinking about my hobbies lately. I'm Kit, by the way, and I'm a professional rock climber and guide. I've been climbing for over 20 years now.
It's great to meet you, Kit! That's an impressive career. What got you interested in rock climbing in the first place?

Well, I grew up near Yosemite, so I was always surrounded by amazing rock faces. I started climbing as a teenager and just never stopped. These days I'm also really into origami - it's such a nice contrast to the physical intensity of climbing.
That's fascinating! How do you find the transition between such physically demanding work and the delicate art of origami?

It's actually a great balance. After a long day on the rocks, folding paper is so calming. I've even started incorporating some of my origami designs into my climbing gear - I made these little paper cranes that I attach to my harness for good luck.
That's a beautiful tradition! Do you have any other hobbies that help you unwind from your climbing career?
Yeah, I've been getting into stand-up comedy lately. I perform at an open mic night once a month at a club downtown. It's terrifying but so much fun - kind of like climbing in that way, actually.
I can see the parallel there! Both require a certain amount of courage. What kind of material do you usually cover in your comedy sets?

Mostly climbing stories and observations about outdoor life. I've got this bit about trying to explain to tourists why they shouldn't feed the squirrels that always gets a good laugh. Oh, and I'm learning Spanish right now - I'm planning to move to Taiwan next year to explore their climbing scene.
That's quite the adventure you're planning! Taiwan for Spanish speakers - that's an interesting choice. What drew you to Taiwan specifically?
